g.data-narration: Welcome to Algorithm Design fundamentals. We explore three essential paradigms: Divide and Conquer, Greedy Method, and Dynamic Programming.
g.text: Three Basic Examples in Algorithm Design
Merge Sort • Knapsack Problem • Longest Common Subsequence

g.data-narration: Three fundamental algorithmic paradigms form the backbone of efficient problem-solving in computer science and engineering applications.
These techniques appear in university exams and real-world applications like scheduling, logistics optimization, and bioinformatics analysis.
Divide and Conquer breaks problems into smaller subproblems, solves each recursively, then combines their solutions into one.
Merge Sort divides the array in half, recursively sorts each half, then merges the sorted halves in O(n) time.
g.text: while i<n1 and j<n2: compare and merge
→ Using Master Theorem: Θ(n log n)

g.data-narration: Tracing Merge Sort on [38,27,43,3,9,82,10]: we divide recursively, sort base cases, then merge sorted subarrays.
Merge Sort is stable, requires O(n) auxiliary space, and works well for linked lists and external sorting applications.
The Greedy method makes locally optimal choices at each step, hoping for a global optimum. It works when greedy-choice property holds.
The Knapsack problem has two variants: 0/1 (pick item once) requires DP, while fractional (pick partial) can use greedy approach.
g.text: Given: n items, weights w₁...wₙ, profits p₁...pₙ,
capacity W. Max profit respecting capacity.
Can take fractions of items (like gold dust).
Algorithm: Sort by profit/weight ratio descending.
Time: O(n log n) due to sorting

g.data-narration: For capacity W=50 with items A(10,60), B(20,100), C(30,120): greedy by ratio gives profit 240 by taking A, B, and 2/3 of C.
Greedy makes immediate decisions without considering future consequences, while DP explores all possibilities storing intermediate results.
g.text: Quick decisions • No backtracking • May not be optimal
Remembers decisions • Explores all possibilities • Always optimal

g.data-narration: Dynamic Programming solves overlapping subproblems by building solutions bottom-up, storing results to avoid redundant computation.
The Longest Common Subsequence finds the longest pattern shared between two strings, crucial for DNA analysis and version control.
g.text: (Subsequence: characters in same order, not necessarily contiguous)

g.data-narration: For X=AGGTAB and Y=GXTXAYB, we build the DP table row by row. The final value L[6][7]=4 gives LCS length of 4 (GTAB).
